Re: 
Your Advice Request of 
11/10/82 
Our No. A-82-188 
Thank you for your recent letter requesting our assistance. 
Without the name(s) of the councilmember(s) in question, we 
cannot provide written advice which will give them immunity 
pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section 18329(b) (copy enclosed). 
However, we will 
endeavor to respond generally to your questions. 
The possibility of financial benefit to either councilmember 
must be measured against the standards established in the 
Commission1s regulation defining "material financial effect" as 
used in Government Code Section 87103. 
Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section 18702 provides that guidance and a copy is 
enclosed. 
As to the park tenant, subdivison (b) (3) (A) would be 
applicable. 
As to the park owner, sUbdivision (b) (1) or (b) (2) 
would apply. 
Assuming that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will 
be a material financial effect on the councilmember(s) in 
question, then the issue will turn on whether the effect on the 
councilmember1s financial interest is distuinguishable from the 
effect on the public generally. (See, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18703, copy enclosed.) 
The Ferraro-and Overstreet Opinions 
issued by the Commission (copies enclosed) are instructive in 
this regard. 


Allen Grimes 
November 24, 19S2 
Page 2 
While we have been provided with no specific facts on the 
"public generally" question, it is unlikely that a mobile home 
park owner will be affected in substantially the same manner as 
a significant segment of the public generally. 
The numerousity 
and diversity requirements established in Ferraro do not appear 
to be present. 
With respect to the park tenant, our advice letter to 
Paul H. Morgan, A-Sl-507, 12/2/S1, which you have not mentioned 
in your request, may provide you with some additional guidance, 
a copy is enclosed for your review. 
However, it should be 
remembered that there is no fixed percentage cut-off for 
determining what constitutes a significant segment of the public 
generally as other factors could influence the cut-off level. 
Lastly, without some specific facts we are unable to assist 
you further in your endeavor to distinguish among our advice 
letters, A-Sl-503, A-S2-036 and A-S2-063. 
If you would like further assistance in this regard, I may 
be reached at (916) 322-5901. 
Sincerely,

Subject: 
Disqualification of a Council Member 
Who is a Mobile Home Park Resident 
Dear Sirs: 
CITY 
POST OFFICE BOX 
A:".ALn"rv, CALIFORNIA 
PHONE: 
466,5678 
FIRE DEPARTMENT 
6005 LEWIS AVENUE 
CALIFORNIA 
466,2141 
We have difficulty distinguishing between the opinions rendered in your Files 
No. A-81"503, A-82-036, and A-82-063. 
We respectfully request an opinion of 
the Commission on the following question: 
A member of the Atascadero City Council resides in a mobile home park located 
on private land within the City. 
There has been presented to the Council by 
a mobile home owners group within the City a proposal and recommendation to 
the Council to enact a mobile home park rent stabilization ordinance. 
While 
the form of the ordinance may vary in its consideration, it is conceivable 
that it could impose a limitation on the increase of mobile home park rents, 
which would appear to be of potential financial benefit to the Council member. 
The question then arises as to whether the Council member may participate in 
discussions on this matter before the Council and vote on any ordinance pro-
posed for action. 
It is conceivable, of course, that any such ordinance would 
not be sustained if challenged in court on the ground that it is unconstitu-
tional because it does not guarantee a fair return to the owner's investment. 
On the other hand, it is conceivable that other members of the Council have 
an ownership interest in a mobile home park located within the City. 
It is 
conceivable that if any rent stabilization ordinance is adopted, those finan-
cial interests would be substantially affected by the adoption of the ordinance 
through reduction in rents. 
Thus, the same questions exists, may such a Council 
member participate and/or vote in an action relating to mobile home park rent 
stabilization. 
Thank you for 
ALLEN GRIMES 
City Attorney 
AG:fr 
assistance. 
\ 


ROBERT J. WILKINS, JR. 
MAYOR

Re: 
City Treasurer of Atascadero 
Dear Janice: 
(80S 
In connection with your study of the matter, I am enclosing for your edifi-
cation a copy of City of Atascadero Ordinance No. 49 and Government Code §§ 
41001, 41002, 41006, 41007, 53630, 53631, 53635, 53637, 53643, 53649, and 
53653, and make reference to the citations to the Public Deposit Act, which 
is found in Government Code § 53600, et seq. 
466·6600 
Our City Finance Director, Ralph Dowell, advised me by telephone yesterday 
that he knows of no existence of a bidding system other than what might be 
contained in the provisions of the Public Deposit Act. 
Mr. Dowell has served 
as the appointed City Treasurer of Atascadero from its incorporation in 1979 
until June of 1982, when Steve Rizzuto was elected City Treasurer. 
Moreover, I do not know of any bidding procedure or guidelines for selecting 
depositories for surplus city funds that have been adopted by the City Council 
since the incorporation of the City. 
Any such guidelines adopted by the City 
Treasurer, of course, would simply be self-serving and subject to change. 
Even if the Council had adopted such guidelines, it has been held in the con-
tract field that the fact that a contract is fair, or even advantageous to 
the city, or has been let to the lowest bidder, is immaterial. 
(Stockton 
Plumbing & Supply Co. v. Wheeler (1924) 68 C.A. 952.) 
Your examination of the provisions of the Public Deposit Act will reveal the 
extent to which discretion has been placed in the City Treasurer with regard 
to the deposit of public funds. 
I call your attention to the annotations of 
decisions and opinions of the Attorney General relating to the Public Deposit 
Act. 
ALLEN GRIMES 
City Attorney

(1305) 466·5678 
jI. 
POLICE DEPARTMENT 
(60S) 
DEPARTMENT 
I have just read a copy of your response to the request for advice by Steven 
M. Rizzuto, City Treasurer of Atascadero, dated August 13, 1982, together with 
a copy of his letter to you dated August 16, 1982. 
I want to say that I heartily agree with your conclusion that the Political 
Reform Act does not prevent Mr. Rizzuto from acting as treasurer while he is 
employed by Fidelity Savings. 
However, I am strongly of the opinion that the 
Act requires Mr. Rizzuto to disqualify himself from making or participating 
in decisions which select Fidelity Savings as a depository of surplus city 
funds. 
I do not believe there is any sound basis upon which it can be contended that 
the treasurer is legally required to select his own depository as a place for 
the deposit of surplus city funds. 
I think it is only honest to advise you that, irrespective of any opinions 
that otherwise might be furnished to Mr. Rizzuto, the above conclusion will 
be the opinion of this office. 
More than anything else, I make this conclu-
sion on the basis of Government Code § 1090 and the common law against unlaw-
ful conflicts of interest, and all of those cases which historically have 
pointed out that a person may not 
serve two masters. 
No amount of 
intellectual jargon can avoid the conclusion that a person should not lawfully 
be allowed to be in the position of selecting his own company as a recipient 
of a benefit from the city when he is treasurer. 
Very truly yours, 
ALLEN GRIMES 
City Attorney 
AG:fr

ORDINANCE NO. 49 
AN ORDINANCE OF THE CITY OF ATASCADERO ADDING 
CHAPTER 5 TO TITLE 2 OF THE ATASCADERO 
MUNICIPAL CODE ESTABLISHING THE 
OFFICE OF CITY TREASURER 
The Atascadero City Council does ordain as follows: 
Section 1. 
Chapter 5 is added to Title 2 of the Atascadero Muni-
cipal Code to read as follows: 
TITLE 2. 
ADMINISTRATION 
Chapter 5. 
City Treasurer -
2-5.201. 
Creation and functions. 
The office of the City Treas-
urer is hereby confirmed as provided in Section 36501 and 36502 of the 
Government Code of the State. 
The City Treasurer shall perform such 
duties as are prescribed by Section 41001 through 41007 of the Govern-
ment Code of the State. 
The City Treasurer shall perform such other 
duties consistent with this Code as may be required of him by the 
Council. 
~-, 
The principal functions of the City Treasurer shall be to receive 
and safely keep all public funds coming into his hands as treasurer, 
and to comply with all laws governing the deposit and securing of pub-
lic funds and the handling of trust funds in his possession. 
2-5.202. 
Deputies. 
The City Treasurer may appoint deputies for 
whose acts he and his bondsmen are responsible, but in doing so, shall 
not inucr any indebtedness or liabiilty on behalf of the City except 
as authorized by the City Council. 
2-5.203. 
Compensation. 
The City Treasurer shall receive such 
compensation and expense allowance as the Council shall from time to 
time determine by resolution or ordinance. 
Such compensation shall be 
a proper charge against such funds of the City as the Council shall 
designate. 
2-5.204. 
Coordination with Director of Finance. 
The City Treas-
urer shall coordinate his activities with the Director of Finance to 
assure availability of funds in response to demands for payment 
against City financal resources. 
The City Treasurer shall not with-
hold making available funds to the Finance Director for payment of 
materials, equipment, goods or services duly authorized by Council 
through the adopted budget or by Council approval by ordinance, reso-
lution or motion when requests for such funds are duly authorized and 
presented by the Director of Finance. 


Ordinance No. 
Treasurer 
2-5.205. 
Investment Policy. 
In addition to the requirement, du-
ties and obligations imposed upon the City Treasurer by the State of 
(' 
California Government Code concerning the investment of City monies, 
the City Treasurer shall attempt to secure the maximum the rate of 
return on invested uncommitted City funds consistent with assuring 
availability of funds to meet the City's financial obligations and 
consistent with statutory investment limitations. 
In order to assure 
compliance with this requirement, the City Treasurer shall closely 
coordinate the investment of funds with the Director of Finance to 
assure the ability of the City to meet its obligations. 
Section 2. 
Ordinance No.6, adopted on July 9, 1979, shall be 
repealed effective with assumption of office by a duly elected City 
Treasurer, but shall constitute transition provisions pending the 
election of such a Treasurer. 
Section 3. 
The City Clerk shall cause this ordinance to be pub-
lished once within fifteen (15) days after its passage in the Atasca-
dero News, a newspaper of general circulation, printed, published and 
circulated in this City in accordance wth Section 36933 of the Govern-
ment Code; shall certify the adoption of this ordinance; and shall 
cause this ordinance and its certification, to be eptered in the Book 
of Ordinances of this City. 
Section 4. 
This ordinance shall go into effect and be in full 
force and effect at 12:01 a.m. on the thirty-first (31st) day after 
passage, but shall not repeal the transition provisions of Ordinance 
( 
No.6. 
The foregoing ordinance was adopted and ordered published at a 
meeting of the Council held on 
February 9 
, 1982, by the 
following vote:

Councilmen Highland, Mackey, Nelson, and Mayor Wilkins 
NOES: 
None 
ABSENT: 
Councilman Stover

90 
ATIORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS 
(VOL. 21 
since the adopted regulation, both are in conflict with the express terms of 
section 60 of the Act. Section 134 is a regulation in excess of the power of the 
Board and is, therefore, void as to that excess. The rule-making power of the 
Board, being derived from section 38 of the Act, is only a power to make rules 
not inconsistent with any of the provisions of the Act itself. Government Code 
section. 11374, which is a codification of the common law rule, states: 
" . . . no regulation adopted is valid or effective unless consistent 
and not in conflict with the statute and reasonably necessary to, effectuate 
the purpose of the statute." (Whitcomb Hotel, Inc. v. California Employ-
ment Commission, 24 Cal. 2d 753; Blatz Brewing Co. v. Collins, 69 Cal. 
App. 2d 639; Tux Ginger Ale Co" Ltd. v. Davis, 12 Cal. App. 2d 73.) 
As stated by the Supreme Court, at page 757, in the Whitcomb Hotel case, 
supra: " ... An administrative officer may nOt make a rule or regulation that 
alters or enlarges the terms of a legislative enactment ...... 
For the foregoing reasons, it is our opinion that section 134 of Title 4 of 
the California Administrative Code, to the extent that it purportS to authorize the 
delivery to an on-sale beer licensee upon a Sunday, is ineffective and invalid. 
~ 
Op;n;on No. 5:;:M'''h 4, 1953 
~ 
", 
SUBJECT: CITY OF MODESTO may lawfully, under its charter, deposit funds 
in a bank of which one of the city Councilmen is a member of the Board of 
Directors and an owner of less than five per cent of the oustanding capital 
Stock. 
Requested by: ASSE.lv!BL YMAN, 30th DISTRICr. 
Opinion by: EDMUND G. BROWN, Attorney General. 
Wjllard A. Shank, Deputy. 
The Honorable Ralph M. Brown, Member of the California Assembly, has 
asked the following question: 
"May the City of Modesto lawfully deposit public funds in a bank 
of which a Modesto City Councilman is a member of the Board of 
Directors and an owner of less than 5 % of the outstanding capital stock 
of the bank?" 
. 
Our conclusions may be summarized as follows: 
The City Treasurer of the City of Modesto may deposit public funds in n 
bank in which a Modesto City Councilman is a director and stockholder owning 
less than 5 % of the outstanding capital stock. 
ANALYSIS 
Government Code section 1090 provides that state, county, and city officers " 
shall not be interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or 
any body or board of which they are members. Violations are punishable as crimes 


MARCH 1953] 
ATfORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS 
91 
and subject the offender to removal from office (Gov. Code, secs. 1097, 36528). 
These provisions are declaratory of rhe well established common law principle of 
public policy which demands that public officers shall be free of personal tempta-
tions in rhe performance of their official duties (Stockton Plumbing and Supply 
Co. v. Wheeler (1924), 68 Cal. App. 592, 229 Pac. 1020). As a general rule, any 
direer or indirect interest of an officer in the subject of a contract with a public 
- -
body is sufficient to taint the contract with illegality, if the interest is such as to 
weer rhe judgment and conduct of the officer either in the making of the contmct 
or its performance (County of Marin v. Messner (1941),44 Cal. App. 2d 577, 
112 Pac. 2d 731). 
In addition to these provisions, the Charter of the City of Modesto (section 
905, Assembly Concurrent Resolution 31, Ch. 46, Stats. 1951) provides that no 
employee of the city shall be financially interested in a contract, sale, purchase or 
lease or transfer of real property to which rhe city is a party or be employed by or 
have an interest in any organization or fum or corporation having a contract with 
rhe city. The Charter provision further provides that an interest of less than 5% 
of rhe stock of a corporation shall not be deemed a "financial interest" under the 
section. 
There roay be some question as to which of rhe above provisions of law is 
applicable in the present case, though the provisions are essentially the same, 
with the exception that a stock interest of less than 5% is not considered culpable 
under the charter section. Since our conclusion would be the same under either 
of the provisions, it is unnecessary to discuss a possible conflict between the 
general law and the charter provision at this time. 
No mention is made in rue Charter for the deposit of public funds of the 
city and the provisions of the Government Code relating to deposit of funds by 
local agencies (sections 53630-53681) would apply (Clouse v. City of SatJ Diego, 
159 Cal. 434, 115 Pac. 573). 
~ 
~ 
The City Treasurer is authorized by sectio~ 53635 of the Government Code to 
deposit public funds in national or state banks and "money so deposited is deemed 
to be in the treasury of the local agency" (section 53636). 
The precise question presented, therefore, is whether the fact that n councilman 
is a direeror and holder of less than a 5 % interest in a bank prohibits the seIeerion 
of that bank as a depository of city funds by the City Treasurer. 
The Attorney General has held that the· fact that a person is a leRisIator does 
not prevent his entry into contracts of sale with the State of California or its 
agencies, except that he may not enter into a contract with the lcRisbture itself 
(14 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 78); that a head court house gardener who owned n 
private nursery was not disqualified from selling nursery supplies to the connty 
of which he was an employee because of the discretion vested in the county pur-
chasing agent to conclude contracts for the county (3 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 188); 
that a County Supervisor is not precluded from contracting for construction work 
with a school district since the contracts for school buildings or school construction 


92 
ATIORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS 
[VOl,. 21 
are let by Boards of School Trustees without control or supervision of the County 
Board of Supervisors (17 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 44). The significant fact in each 
of these opinions is the independent starus of the parry contracting on behalf of 
the governmental agency. 
The selection of the depositOry in the instant case is in the discretion of the 
Ciry Treasurer and not subject to the supervision or control of the City Council. 
It is true that the council is responsible for the appointment and removal of the 
Treasurer, but the relationship between one member of the council and the 
Treasurer is no less remote than that of a legislatOr and an agency ~f the State 
Government. 
Weare of the opinion that the public funds of the Ciry of ModestO may be 
deposited by the Ciry Treasurer in a bank in which a councilman of the ciry is a 
directOr and a stOckholder with less than 5 % of the outstanding capital stock . 
.-.... 
Opinion No. 52-175-March 5, 1953 
SUBJECT: STATE EMPLOYEES' RETIREMENT SYSTEM-Members of, already 
retired under, are not barred from service of the State or of a contracting 
agency, provided that they meet the requirements of reinstatement from 
retirement; if such reinstatement is effected, such employee may be paid and 
accept salary or emoluments of office with the state or the contraCting agency. 
Requested by: BOARD OF ADMINISTRATION, STATE EMPLOYEES' 
RETIREMENT SYSTEM. 
Opinion by: EDMUND G. BROWN, Attorney General. 
John Morris, ,Deputy. 
The Board of Administration of the State Employees' Retirement System has 
asked whether one retired under the State Employees' Retirement System may be 
elected to office in the service of the State or of a contracting agency, and if elected, 
whether he may be paid the salary of such office. 
The conclusions reached may be summarized as follows: 
If otherwise qualified, a person retired under the State Employees' Retirement 
System is not barred from office by retirement alone, provid<.'1l that he meet the 
requirements of reinstatement from retirement; and, reinstatement effected and 
retirement allowance terminated, he may be paid and accept the salary or emolu-
ments of office. 
ANALYSIS 
SeCtion 20060 of the Government Code was amended in 1949 to read as 
follows: "Except as provided in section 21300 a person shall not be paid for any 
service rendered by him to the State or contraCting agency after his retirement is. 
effective and while he is entitled to receive a retirement allowance under this 
system."

re: 
Your Letter of Advice, No. A-82-124 
Dear Ms. McLean: 
I appreciate the manner and clarity in which you responded to my 
previous letter requesting advice. 
After discussing your letter 
with you today, I believe that we should look further into the 
application of Government Code Section 18701, as I believe I am 
legally required to participate in the decision process which is 
subject to our discussion of "conflict of interest". 
I am enclosing for your review Ordinance No. 49 of the City of 
Atascadero, adding Chapter 5 to title 2 of the Atascadero Municipal 
Code which establ ished the office of City Treasurer. 
Further, 
I believe there exists no means at my disposal to secure an 
independent alternative source of decision consistant with the 
purposes and terms of the statute and ordinance authorizing the 
decision. 
As you suggested in your response letter, paragraph 9, the bidding 
system removes most of my involvement in determining whether Fidelity 
Savings, or any other association, is chosen as one of the institutions 
to be used for our investment of reserve public funds, and my contractual 
arrangements with all depositories are principly ministrel. 
However, 
the investment policy as established in Title 2 - 5.205 of the 
Atascadero Municipal Code does require the Treasurer's attempt to secure 
the maximum rete of return on invested uncommitted City funds and 
more broadly Government Code 53637. and 53682. 
Subjective evaluation 
on the part of the Treasurer will enter into the decision process if, 
in the opinion of the Treasurer, the deposit of funds will no be to 
the public advantage. 
In this si tuation, security of deposited funds 
may be taken into consideration and 'rate v. risk' judgement may be 
invoked. 
Under Atas. 2-5.202, and GC 41007, any deputy shall hold 
office at the pleasure of the city treasurer .•• which I contrue to mean 
to follow the policy established by code, ordinance. and pol icy then 
established by the Treasurer. 
7109 EL CAMINO REAL. ATASCADERO, CALIFORNIA 93422-9990 • 805-466-6550 
Board of Directors: Harold R. Walt, Father Timothy L. McDonnell, S.J., J. Ralph Stone, Edward H. Barker, Sherman Miller 


Janis McLean 
August 16, 1982 
page 2. 
I hope that this information will be of further use to you in 
helping determine the ultimate question as to whether I can 
effectively participate in the decision making process in 
the investing innactive public reserves. 
Should you have need for further information, please feel free 
to contact me. 
I have supplied our city attorney, Allen Grimes, 
with copies of your letter dated August 13, 1982, and this 
additional request. 
_~iaIIY

ORDINANCE NO. 49 
AN ORDINANCE OF THE CITY OF ATASCADERO ADDING 
CHAPTER 5 TO TITLE 2 OF THE ATASCADERO 
MUNICIPAL CODE ESTABLISHING THE 
OFFICE OF CITY TREASURER 
,The Atascadero City Council does ordain as follows: 
Section 1. 
Chapter 5 is added to Title 2 of the Atascadero Muni-
cipal Code to read as follows: 
TITLE 2. 
ADMINISTRATION 
Chapter 5. 
City Treasurer -
2-5.201. 
Creation and functions. 
The office of the City Treas-
urer is hereby confirmed as provided in Section 36501 and 36502 of the 
Government Code of the State. 
The City Treasurer shall perform such 
duties as are prescribed by Section 41001 through 41007 of the Govern-
ment Code of the State. 
The City Treasurer shall perform such other 
duties consistent with this Code as may be required of him by the 
Council. 
r:.:~'.-
The pr inc ipal functions of the Ci ty Treasurer shall be to rece i ve 
\i~); 
and safely keep all public funds corning into his hands as treasurer, 
and to comply with all laws governing the deposit and securing of pub-
lic funds and the handling of trust funds in his possession. 
2-5.202. 
Deputies. 
The City Treasurer may appoint deputies for 
whose acts he and his bondsmen are responsible, but in doing so, shall 
not inucr any indebtedness or liabiilty on behalf of the City except 
as authorized by the City Council. 
2-5.203. 
Compensation. 
The City Treasurer shall receive such 
compensation and expense allowance as the Council shall from time- to 
time determine by resolution or ordinance. 
Such compensation shall be 
a proper charge against such funds of the City as the Council shall 
designate. 
2-5.204. 
Coordination with Director of Finance. 
The City Treas-
urer shall coordinate his activities with the Director of Finance to 
assure availability of funds in response to demands for payment 
against City financal resources. 
The City Treasurer shall not with-
hold making available funds to the Finance Director for payment of 
materials, equipment, goods or services duly authorized by Council 
through the adopted budget or by Council approval by ord\nance, reso-
lution or motion when requests for such funds are duly authorized and 
presented by the Director of Finance. 


Ordinance No. 
- City Treasurer 
2-5.205. 
Investment Policy. 
In addition to the requirement, du-
ties and obligations imposed upon the City Treasurer by the State of' 
California Government Code concerning the investment of City monies, 
the City Treasurer shall attempt to secure the maximum the rate of 
return on invested uncommitted City funds consistent with assu~ing 
availability of funds to meet the City's financial obligations and 
consistent with statutory investment limitations. 
In order to assure 
compliance with this requirement, the City Treasurer shall closely 
coordinate the investment of funds with the Director of Finance to 
assure the ability of the City to meet its obligations. 
Section 2. 
Ordinance No.6, adopted on July 9, 1979, shall be 
repealed effective with assumption of office by a duly elected City 
Treasurer, but shall constitute transition provisions pending the 
election of such a Treasurer. 
Section 3. 
The City Clerk shall cause this ordinance to be pub-
lished once within fifteen (15) days after·its passage in the Atasca-
dero News, a newspaper of general circulation, printed, published and 
circulated in this City in accordance wth Section 36933 of the Govern-
ment Code; shall certify the adoption of this ordinance; and shall 
cause this ordinance and its certification, to be entered in the Book 
of Ordinances of this' City. 
Section 4. 
This ordinance shall go into effect and be in full 
force and effect at 12:01 a.m. on the thirty-first (31st) day after 
passage, but shall not repeal the transition provisions of Ordinance 
No.6. 
The foregoing ordinance was adopted and ordered published at a 
meeting of the Council held on 
February 9 
, 1982, by the 
following vote: 
AYES: 
Councilmen Highland, Mackey, Nelson, and Mayor Wilkins 
NOES: 
None 
ABSENT: 
Councilman Stover

sections.question:
, then the issue will turn on whether the effect on the 
councilmember1s financial interest is distuinguishable from the 
effect on the public generally. (See, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18703, copy enclosed.) 
The Ferraro-and Overstreet Opinions 
issued by the Commission (copies enclosed) are instructive in 
this regard. 


Allen Grimes 
November 24, 19S2  
While we have been provided with no specific facts on the 
"public generally" question, it is unlikely that a mobile home 
park owner will be affected in substantially the same manner as 
a significant segment of the public generally. 
The numerousity 
and diversity requirements established in Ferraro do not appear 
to be present. 
With respect to the park tenant, our advice letter to 
Paul H. Morgan, A-Sl-507, 12/2/S1, which you have not mentioned 
in your request, may provide you with some additional guidance, 
a copy is enclosed for your review. 
However, it should be 
remembered that there is no fixed percentage cut-off for 
determining what constitutes a significant segment of the public 
generally as other factors could influence the cut-off level. 
Lastly, without some specific facts we are unable to assist 
you further in your endeavor to distinguish among our advice 
letters, A-Sl-503, A-S2-036 and A-S2-063. 
If you would like further assistance in this regard, I may 
be reached at (916) 322-5901.
sections.conclusion: Without the name(s) of the councilmember(s) in question, we cannot provide written advice which will give them immunity pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b). However, the possibility of financial benefit must be measured against the standards established in the Commission's regulation defining 'material financial effect' as used in Government Code Section 87103 (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702). For a park tenant, subdivision (b)(3)(A) would be applicable; for a park owner, subdivision (b)(1) or (b)(2) would apply. Assuming a material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable, the issue turns on whether the effect on the councilmember's financial interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703). It is unlikely that a mobile home park owner will be affected in substantially the same manner as a significant segment of the public generally, as the numerosity and diversity requirements do not appear to be present.
sections.analysis:
Government Code section 1090 provides that state, county, and city officers " 
shall not be interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or 
any body or board of which they are members. Violations are punishable as crimes 


MARCH 1953] 
ATfORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS 
91 
and subject the offender to removal from office (Gov. Code, secs. 1097, 36528). 
These provisions are declaratory of rhe well established common law principle of 
public policy which demands that public officers shall be free of personal tempta-
tions in rhe performance of their official duties (Stockton Plumbing and Supply 
Co. v. Wheeler (1924), 68 Cal. App. 592, 229 Pac. 1020). As a general rule, any 
direer or indirect interest of an officer in the subject of a contract with a public 
- -
body is sufficient to taint the contract with illegality, if the interest is such as to 
weer rhe judgment and conduct of the officer either in the making of the contmct 
or its performance (County of Marin v. Messner (1941),44 Cal. App. 2d 577, 
112 Pac. 2d 731). 
In addition to these provisions, the Charter of the City of Modesto (section 
905, Assembly Concurrent Resolution 31, Ch. 46, Stats. 1951) provides that no 
employee of the city shall be financially interested in a contract, sale, purchase or 
lease or transfer of real property to which rhe city is a party or be employed by or 
have an interest in any organization or fum or corporation having a contract with 
rhe city. The Charter provision further provides that an interest of less than 5% 
of rhe stock of a corporation shall not be deemed a "financial interest" under the 
section. 
There roay be some question as to which of rhe above provisions of law is 
applicable in the present case, though the provisions are essentially the same, 
with the exception that a stock interest of less than 5% is not considered culpable 
under the charter section. Since our conclusion would be the same under either 
of the provisions, it is unnecessary to discuss a possible conflict between the 
general law and the charter provision at this time. 
No mention is made in rue Charter for the deposit of public funds of the 
city and the provisions of the Government Code relating to deposit of funds by 
local agencies (sections 53630-53681) would apply (Clouse v. City of SatJ Diego, 
159 Cal. 434, 115 Pac. 573). 
~ 
~ 
The City Treasurer is authorized by sectio~ 53635 of the Government Code to 
deposit public funds in national or state banks and "money so deposited is deemed 
to be in the treasury of the local agency" (section 53636). 
The precise question presented, therefore, is whether the fact that n councilman 
is a direeror and holder of less than a 5 % interest in a bank prohibits the seIeerion 
of that bank as a depository of city funds by the City Treasurer. 
The Attorney General has held that the· fact that a person is a leRisIator does 
not prevent his entry into contracts of sale with the State of California or its 
agencies, except that he may not enter into a contract with the lcRisbture itself 
(14 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 78); that a head court house gardener who owned n 
private nursery was not disqualified from selling nursery supplies to the connty 
of which he was an employee because of the discretion vested in the county pur-
chasing agent to conclude contracts for the county (3 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 188); 
that a County Supervisor is not precluded from contracting for construction work 
with a school district since the contracts for school buildings or school construction 


92 
ATIORNEY GENERAL'S OPINIONS 
[VOl,. 21 
are let by Boards of School Trustees without control or supervision of the County 
Board of Supervisors (17 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 44). The significant fact in each 
of these opinions is the independent starus of the parry contracting on behalf of 
the governmental agency. 
The selection of the depositOry in the instant case is in the discretion of the 
Ciry Treasurer and not subject to the supervision or control of the City Council. 
It is true that the council is responsible for the appointment and removal of the 
Treasurer, but the relationship between one member of the council and the 
Treasurer is no less remote than that of a legislatOr and an agency ~f the State 
Government. 
Weare of the opinion that the public funds of the Ciry of ModestO may be 
deposited by the Ciry Treasurer in a bank in which a councilman of the ciry is a 
directOr and a stOckholder with less than 5 % of the outstanding capital stock . 
.-....

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city council member who resides in a mobile home park may participate in and vote on a proposed rent stabilization ordinance that could financially benefit them, and whether the same rules apply to council members who own mobile home parks that could be negatively affected.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC cannot provide immunity without specific councilmember names, but advises that disqualification depends on whether the financial effect is material and distinguishable from effects on the general public. A mobile home park owner likely does not meet the public-effect exception, suggesting disqualification would be required.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document contains multiple letters and supporting materials. Main advice letter dated November 24, 1982 (A-82-188) is the primary FPPC response. Earlier acknowledgment letter dated November 17, 1982 and incoming request dated November 10, 1982 are also included. Additional correspondence regarding City Treasurer conflict of interest issues and Ordinance No. 49 are included but relate to separate matters. OCR quality varies throughout document.

18703, copy enclosed.) 
The Ferraro-and Overstreet Opinions 
issued by the Commission (copies enclosed) are instructive in

Allen Grimes 
November 24, 19S2  
While we have been provided with no specific facts on the 
"public generally" question, it is unlikely that a mobile home 
park owner will be affected in substantially the same manner as 
a significant segment of the public generally.

CONCLUSION: Without the name(s) of the councilmember(s) in question, we cannot provide written advice which will give them immunity pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b). However, the possibility of financial benefit must be measured against the standards established in the Commission's regulation defining 'material financial effect' as used in Government Code Section 87103 (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702). For a park tenant, subdivision (b)(3)(A) would be applicable; for a park owner, subdivision (b)(1) or (b)(2) would apply. Assuming a material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable, the issue turns on whether the effect on the councilmember's financial interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703). It is unlikely that a mobile home park owner will be affected in substantially the same manner as a significant segment of the public generally, as the numerosity and diversity requirements do not appear to be present.
embedding.first_500_words: P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance (916) 322·5662 Allen Grimes City Attorney City of Atascadero P.O. Box 749 Atascadero, CA 93423 Dear Mr. Grimes: Administration 322.5660 Executive/legal 322.5901 Enforcement 322-6441 November 24, 1982 Statements of Economic Interest 322-6444 Re: Your Advice Request of 11/10/82 Our No. A-82-188 Thank you for your recent letter requesting our assistance. Without the name(s) of the councilmember(s) in question, we cannot provide written advice which will give them immunity pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b) (copy enclosed). However, we will endeavor to respond generally to your questions. The possibility of financial benefit to either councilmember must be measured against the standards established in the Commission1s regulation defining "material financial effect" as used in Government Code Section 87103. Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 provides that guidance and a copy is enclosed. As to the park tenant, subdivison (b) (3) (A) would be applicable. As to the park owner, sUbdivision (b) (1) or (b) (2) would apply. Assuming that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will be a material financial effect on the councilmember(s) in question, then the issue will turn on whether the effect on the councilmember1s financial interest is distuinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (See, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703, copy enclosed.) The Ferraro-and Overstreet Opinions issued by the Commission (copies enclosed) are instructive in this regard. Allen Grimes November 24, 19S2 Page 2 While we have been provided with no specific facts on the "public generally" question, it is unlikely that a mobile home park owner will be affected in substantially the same manner as a significant segment of the public generally. The numerousity and diversity requirements established in Ferraro do not appear to be present. With respect to the park tenant, our advice letter to Paul H. Morgan, A-Sl-507, 12/2/S1, which you have not mentioned in your request, may provide you with some additional guidance, a copy is enclosed for your review. However, it should be remembered that there is no fixed percentage cut-off for determining what constitutes a significant segment of the public generally as other factors could influence the cut-off level. Lastly, without some specific facts we are unable to assist you further in your endeavor to distinguish among our advice letters, A-Sl-503, A-S2-036 and A-S2-063. If you would like further assistance in this regard, I may be reached at (916) 322-5901. Sincerely, '~-~ I -/- <'>2' /. c· ! Y-{I v'-.-> / -> - C .,>- Robert E. ldigh ) Counsel . embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to City of Atascadero's inquiry regarding whether council members with financial interests in mobile home parks (as residents or owners) may participate in voting on a proposed rent stabilization ordinance. The FPPC provides general guidance referencing applicable regulations and prior opinions while declining to give specific advice without identifying the councilmembers involved.
